messages.author: Delltar
messages.text: This is a bit of a wild idea maybe, but I noticed that in some locations (like transmitter towers or particularly located buildings) people have to use multiple repeaters to prevent one side from being in a shadow. Situation where there must be two repeaters on a single transmitter tower probably isn't ideal and so I wanted to float the (maybe a bit ambitious) idea of possibly connecting multiple repeater nodes into one logical repeater. Now the reality is probably complicated for this, I am not aware of any existing LoRa hardware that would have multiple LoRa modules to accommodate multiple antennas. It might also not be practical to actually have such HW since in reality the "multi antenna repeater" would need to have some distance between the antennas and leading all of them to one box is probably not efficient. Connecting multiple physical nodes to create one logical repeater might also be a tall order, especially if those nodes run from solar where maintaining active wifi connection, or running some sort of central devices to coordinate everything might not be practical. Though maybe connecting multiple nodes to raspberry pi via BLE and powering the pi from solar is not so far fetched. So as you can see, this is more idle musing from me rather than some concrete idea, but wanted to float it anyway
messages.content_html: This is a bit of a wild idea maybe, but I noticed that in some locations (like transmitter towers or particularly located buildings) people have to use multiple repeaters to prevent one side from being in a shadow. Situation where there must be two repeaters on a single transmitter tower probably isn't ideal and so I wanted to float the (maybe a bit ambitious) idea of possibly connecting multiple repeater nodes into one logical repeater. Now the reality is probably complicated for this, I am not aware of any existing LoRa hardware that would have multiple LoRa modules to accommodate multiple antennas. It might also not be practical to actually have such HW since in reality the "multi antenna repeater" would need to have some distance between the antennas and leading all of them to one box is probably not efficient. Connecting multiple physical nodes to create one logical repeater might also be a tall order, especially if those nodes run from solar where maintaining active wifi connection, or running some sort of central devices to coordinate everything might not be practical. Though maybe connecting multiple nodes to raspberry pi via BLE and powering the pi from solar is not so far fetched. So as you can see, this is more idle musing from me rather than some concrete idea, but wanted to float it anyway

messages.author: alaspooryorick
messages.text: I think this is a pretty worthwile request if extremely specific when needed. Avoiding pollution of bandwidth by many repeaters around large objects or installations (mountain, skyscraper, buildings, etc) is a pretty much only a net benefit, and would prevent multiplication of hops across such a range

Avoiding pollution of bandwidth by many repeaters around large objects or installations (mountain, skyscraper, buildings, etc) is a pretty much only a net benefit, and would prevent multiplication of hops across such a range

messages.author: Scott
messages.text: Yeah, the transports codes are a filtering mechanism which repeaters will interrogate. They will be for 2 purposes, 1) for limiting flood broadcasts to just a subset (ie. sub-mesh) of repeaters, 2) enabling completely private repeater infra. The codes will go thru an algo that takes a Transport Key + packet info -> transport code. For private infra the tranport keys are only disclosed to participating nodes. For 1) these will be like the #channel names, where the Transport Key is auto-generated, ie. public knowledge.
messages.content_html: Yeah, the transports codes are a filtering mechanism which repeaters will interrogate. They will be for 2 purposes, 1) for limiting flood broadcasts to just a subset (ie. sub-mesh) of repeaters, 2) enabling completely private repeater infra. The codes will go thru an algo that takes a Transport Key + packet info -> transport code. For private infra the tranport keys are only disclosed to participating nodes. For 1) these will be like the #channel names, where the Transport Key is auto-generated, ie. public knowledge.
messages.reply_html:
Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
scott am I completely off base with my guess as to how this will work?

messages.author: Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
messages.text: @Scott repeaters using a specific transport code will all need to be adjacent to one another to form a "sub-mesh" correct?
messages.content_html: @Scott repeaters using a specific transport code will all need to be adjacent to one another to form a "sub-mesh" correct?

messages.author: Scott
messages.text: Typically, yes, but can be whatever you want. At present for case 1) I'm calling them #regions. These will be completely up to local communities to agree on. You could have #Seattle, for instance. Or smaller areas, like #Seattle-Metro. A repeater will be able to be configured for multiple #regions. So, it could have those two for instance. Or if it's near boundary, then both neighboring #regions.
messages.content_html: Typically, yes, but can be whatever you want. At present for case 1) I'm calling them #regions. These will be completely up to local communities to agree on. You could have #Seattle, for instance. Or smaller areas, like #Seattle-Metro. A repeater will be able to be configured for multiple #regions. So, it could have those two for instance. Or if it's near boundary, then both neighboring #regions.

messages.author: psfshr
messages.text: Questions... Will transport codes be applied on the client end, or by a repeater when it receives a packet? Thinking about people with mobile clients roaming between regions. Assuming repeaters won't route packets between transport codes, i.e. if you have a repeater in two regions it won't pass packets between them? Assuming this is mainly for "channel" flooding? Direct messages will still transit the mesh as normal including flood? Will Public get a Transport Key so you can limit it's flooding?
messages.content_html: Questions... Will transport codes be applied on the client end, or by a repeater when it receives a packet? Thinking about people with mobile clients roaming between regions. Assuming repeaters won't route packets between transport codes, i.e. if you have a repeater in two regions it won't pass packets between them? Assuming this is mainly for "channel" flooding? Direct messages will still transit the mesh as normal including flood? Will Public get a Transport Key so you can limit it's flooding?
messages.reply_html:
Scott
Yeah, the transports codes are a filtering mechanism which repeaters will interrogate. They will be for 2 purposes, 1) for limiting flood broadcasts to just a subset (ie. sub-mesh) of repeaters, 2) enabling completely private repeater infra. The codes will go thru an algo that takes a Transport Key + packet info -> transport code. For private infra the tranport keys are only disclosed to participating nodes. For 1) these will be like the #channel names, where the Transport Key is auto-generated, ie. public knowledge.

messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: I've been thinking along the same lines. Now that the UK has changed to narrow 62.5 KHz bandwidth, that allows 4 channels in our tiny 868 MHz frequency allocation. Multi radio opens up potential for h ighly direction backhaul antennas in a coordinated & planner repeater cluster.
messages.content_html: I've been thinking along the same lines. Now that the UK has changed to narrow 62.5 KHz bandwidth, that allows 4 channels in our tiny 868 MHz frequency allocation. Multi radio opens up potential for h ighly direction backhaul antennas in a coordinated & planner repeater cluster.

messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: Have you looked at how Thread works? I like the concepts about how nodes join a network and how the network finds the physical location of a node by using multiple addresses. It seems very well thought through but I fear the bandwidth needs for network state are too high for LoRa. But some inspiration could be gleaned, perhaps.
messages.content_html: Have you looked at how Thread works? I like the concepts about how nodes join a network and how the network finds the physical location of a node by using multiple addresses. It seems very well thought through but I fear the bandwidth needs for network state are too high for LoRa. But some inspiration could be gleaned, perhaps.
